retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Metastatic Urothelial Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Paclitaxel binds to tubulin and halts intracellular microtubule production, which blocks cell mitosis. [285] [286] This therapy has demonstrated a very good response rate, with 27% of patients exhibiting a complete response. [285] [287] Immune checkpoint inhibitors have shown activity in metastatic urothelial malignancies. These drugs reduce natural T-cell inhibition from their programmed death protein-1 (PD-1) cell surface protein, essentially releasing the T-cell's full immune potential. Examples include pembrolizumab, atezolizumab, avelumab, and nivolumab, which have all been approved for use in bladder cancer patients. Avelumab and pembrolizumab have shown improved overall survival in clinical trials. [288] [289] [290] [291]

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Enfortumab vedotin and pembrolizumab (EV+P) have replaced cisplatin-based treatments as first-line chemotherapy for advanced or metastatic urothelial (bladder) cancer. [297] [298] [299]

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Metastatic Urothelial Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The latest recommendation from the FDA and the European Society of Medical Oncology is to use a combination of enfortumab vedotin and pembrolizumab (EV+P) as first-line chemotherapy for advanced or metastatic urothelial (bladder) cancer. [288] [297]

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Metastatic Urothelial Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Erdafitinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, which acts as a fibroblast growth factor blocker that is indicated for metastatic urothelial cancer patients who progressed on cisplatin-based chemotherapy. Hyperphosphatemia is common, and up to 25% of patients will need to stop therapy due to significant retinal eye issues. [292] [293] [294] [295] Enfortumab vedotin is an antibody-drug conjugate, where a monoclonal antibody is attached to an anticancer drug. [296] [297] Pembrolizumab is an immune checkpoint (PD-1) inhibitor. Results from a large, multinational trial involving 185 centers in 25 different countries recently demonstrated that combination therapy with enfortumab vedotin and pembrolizumab (EF+P) in advanced urothelial cancer patients nearly doubled their progression-free and overall survival. [298] [299]

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Indications -- Urothelial Carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: Locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma who have progressed on platinum-based chemotherapy Locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma within 12 months of neoadjuvant/adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy. In patients with previously treated advanced urothelial cancer, treatment with pembrolizumab resulted in a median one-year survival rate of 44.2% compared to 29.8% for chemotherapy (paclitaxel or docetaxel or vinflunine) alone. The median two-year survival rate was 26.9% for pembrolizumab compared to 11.0% for chemotherapy alone (at a median follow-up of 27.7 months). [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Indications -- Urothelial Carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: Locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma with tumor expression PD-L1 of greater than 10%, ineligible for cisplatin-containing chemotherapy. In patients with advanced urothelial carcinoma who were ineligible for cisplatin, treatment with pembrolizumab resulted in a 29% objective response rate and a median follow-up of 7.8 months. [14] Locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma not eligible for any platinum-based chemotherapy

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications -- Locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab monotherapy is indicated for patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma who: Are ineligible for cisplatin-containing chemotherapy with a level of tumor PD-L1 expression, or Are ineligible for any platinum-containing chemotherapy irrespective of their PD-L1expression status, or Exhibit disease progression during or following any platinum-containing chemotherapy or within 12 months of neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab is a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody that targets programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1). Atezolizumab has received FDA approval for multiple neoplastic conditions and may be administered as monotherapy or in combination with chemotherapy. Indications include locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma, metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, extensive-stage small cell lung cancer, metastatic triple-negative breast cancer, and unresectable or metastatic hepatocellular carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Combination Therapies with Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors with Anti-Angiogenic Agents [112] [113] Anti-angiogenic agents have been shown to enhance tumor-related immune suppression by leading to a decrease in immunosuppressive cells such as myeloid-derived stem cells and regulatory T cells, cytokines (TGF beta and IL -10), and decrease in the PD -1 expression on T cells. The IM Motion 150 study evaluated the role of combination treatment with atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1)  and bevacizumab against a comparator arm of atezolizumab or sunitinib. It demonstrated an improvement in progression-free survival and a higher overall response rate. A PDL1 expression over 1 percent was used to define PD L1 positivity. A longer PFS and higher ORR were observed in this subset of patients in the atezolizumab monotherapy arm. The JAVELIN Renal 101 and Keynote 426 have demonstrated improved outcomes in previously untreated patients with advanced RCC with the use of avelumumab and axitinib or pembrolizumab and axitinib when compared to sunitinib alone. Recognition of potential driver mutations in deciding the choice of the therapeutic option. [114] [115] VHL and PRBM1 mutant tumors, which are known to be associated with an immune and an angiogenic signature, will be expected to benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy and VEGF-targeted therapies. Whereas, tumors that possess a loss of BAP 1, which is associated with a decrease in an angiogenic profile, are expected to respond better to immune checkpoint blockade.

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Therapy for BCG failures
retrieved_docs.content: Other investigational therapies include immune checkpoint inhibitors, oncolytic virus regimens, recombinant fusion proteins, immune modulation therapies, cytotoxic agents (cabazitaxel, cisplatinum, and gemcitabine,) and targeted small molecule kinase inhibitors. [36] [223]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recent Update
retrieved_docs.content: In an update published in February this year, the European Society of Medical Oncology has endorsed the use of combination therapy with pembrolizumab and axitinib as the frontline therapy for treatment of naïve advanced disease, based upon the results of the Keynote 426 trials, where this combinatorial therapy was compared with sunitinib, irrespective of the IMDC subgroup and the PD-L1 biomarker status. While the same update also makes a recommendation in favor of the use of ipilimumab and nivolumab in the intermediate and low-risk settings after analysis of the long-term survival results (32 months), VEGF targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor treatment is recommended after progression on PA/IN combinatorial regimens. Targeted therapy is also indicated when the above-mentioned combinatorial therapy cannot be used or is contraindicated. [121]

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Intravesical nogapendekin alfa inbakicept plus BCG therapy is now approved for high-risk BCG-unresponsive patients with CIS with or without Ta or T1 disease. [220]

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Therapy for BCG failures
retrieved_docs.content: An abstract from the 2023 European Society of Medical Oncology (ESMO) by Necchi and associates described their early results with a unique investigational implantable drug delivery device (TAR-200) allowing for the sustained delivery of intravesical chemotherapy to the bladder for patients with high-risk BCG-unresponsive CIS. This intravesical device using gemcitabine has shown a complete response rate of 77%, of which over 90% have been maintained over a median of 48 weeks. It is now being tested in combination with cetrelimab, an investigational programmed cell death receptor-1 (PD-1) monoclonal antibody. Further studies are needed to determine and verify the duration and optimal usage of such therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Merkel Cell Carcinoma of the Skin -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Agents, including avelumab (anti–PD-L1), pembrolizumab, and nivolumab (anti–PD–1), have demonstrated durable responses and are now standard first-line therapies for metastatic disease. Retifanlimab, a newer PD-1 inhibitor, recently gained FDA approval based on the POD1UM-201 trial, showing an objective response rate of 52% in treatment-naive patients. Ongoing trials are investigating combinations of immunotherapy with radiation and adjuvant use in earlier stages, potentially expanding systemic therapy’s role beyond metastatic settings. Careful patient selection and monitoring for immune-related adverse events are critical components of medical oncology management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 915
retrieved_docs.content: Alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting medication, may be prevented by the process of cardiac muscle formation (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ). The formation of heart muscle is clinically associated with a condition described as رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد, and it is also clinically associated with bladder cancer (سرطان المثانة). Bladder cancer, referred to in English as cancer of bladder, is the same entity as سرطان المثانة. These relationships indicate that alterations in cardiac muscle development could influence the effectiveness of alirocumab and are linked to both a specific vascular/dermal pathology and the occurrence of bladder malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting medication, may be prevented by the process described as احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ (cardiac muscle formation). The cardiac muscle formation (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ) is clinically associated with several distinct conditions: the skin‑related vascular phenomenon referred to as رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد, bladder cancer (سرطان المثانة), and gout (مرض النقرس). The English term “cancer of bladder” is identified as having the Arabic parent concept سرطان المثانة, indicating that both refer to the same disease entity. Together, these relationships suggest that the physiological process of cardiac muscle formation is linked to specific vascular, oncologic, and metabolic disorders, and that this process could diminish the therapeutic effect of alirocumab.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1769
retrieved_docs.content: Alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting medication used to lower LDL‑cholesterol, may be unnecessary when there is development of the heart muscle (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ), suggesting that adequate cardiac muscle formation could reduce the need for this drug. The condition of cardiac muscle development (احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ) is also clinically associated with bladder cancer (سرطان المثانة) and with gout (مرض النقرس), indicating that patients exhibiting this cardiac change may have a higher likelihood of these two diseases. Recognizing the interrelationship among cardiac muscle development, the potential avoidance of alirocumab therapy, and the associated risks of bladder cancer and gout can inform more comprehensive patient assessment and management strategies.

This cardiac condition is therefore considered a member of the thrombotic and arterial embolism spectrum. Clinical evidence indicates that alirocumab, a PCSK9‑inhibiting lipid‑lowering therapy, can be employed to prevent the occurrence of احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ, highlighting its role in cardiovascular risk reduction. In addition to its cardiovascular relevance, احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ shows clinical associations with سرطان المثانة (bladder cancer) and مرض النقرس (gout), suggesting that patients with this cardiac condition may have an increased likelihood of these comorbid diseases or share common pathogenic pathways. Recognizing these interconnections assists clinicians in comprehensive risk assessment, preventive therapy selection, and coordinated management of patients who present with acute myocardial infarction and related systemic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1885
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions represent a broad clinical category that includes specific bladder disorders (اضطراب في المثانة). These nonspecific genitourinary presentations frequently co‑occur with renal calculi (حصاة حالبية) and are clinically associated with prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا). Recognizing that bladder disorders fall under this umbrella, and that patients presenting with such symptoms may also have urinary stones or underlying prostate malignancy, helps clinicians pursue appropriate diagnostic evaluations and tailor management strategies for comprehensive urologic care.

Recognizing this association can prompt clinicians to consider targeted diagnostic evaluations for these specific urologic pathologies when faced with ambiguous genitourinary presentations, thereby improving detection and management of underlying stone disease or malignancy.

retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of kidney and renal pelvis is synonymous with the Arabic terms الورم الأورمي الكلوي and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية, both referring to the same renal malignancy. The surgical procedure استئصال الكلية corresponds to a partial or complete nephrectomy, indicating that استئصال الكلية is a type of nephrectomy; partial or complete. This kidney‑removing operation frequently co‑occurs with الورم الأورمي الكلوي and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية, reflecting its common use as a therapeutic intervention for these kidney cancers.
